children.title: A New 7-Transistor SRAM Cell Design with High Read Stability
children.metadata: Static Random Access Memory (SRAM); Static Noise Margin (SNG); Seven-transistor SRAM (7T); Noise Immunity; Data Access Mechanism; Double Word Operation (DWord)
children.content: Yen Hsiang Tseng, Yimeng Zhang, Leona Okamura and Tsutomu Yoshihara<|txt_split|>Graduate School of Information, Production and Systems, Waseda University, Fukuoka, 808-0135, Japan<|txt_split|>Email: yimeng.zhang@fuji.waseda.jp<|txt_split|>Abstract-The conventional SRAMs, namely four-transistor SRAM (4T) and six-transistor SRAM (6T), suffered from the external noise, because they have direct paths through the external impedance. In this study, we have studied a seven-transistor (7T) SRAM which has no direct path through BL to the data storage nodes and has higher endurance against external noise. The proposed cell is composed of two separate data access mechanisms; one is a 12-bit double word operation (DWord) and the other is based upon our SRAM design, data destruction never occurs in the read operation. Simulation result shows that the read Static-Noise-Margin (SMM) of the proposed cell was enhanced by LAX and 8.31X with the conventional 4T and 6T systems. This results also manufactured a cell and confirmed its performance.<|txt_split|>Keywords—Static Random Access Memory (SRAM); Static Noise Margin (SNG)

children.title: I. INTRODUCTION
children.metadata: SRAM; SoC; 4T SRAM; stability; noise resistance; CMOS technology; performance improvement; read/write/standby cycles; simulation results
children.content: With the advantages of high speed and ease of use, static random access memory (SRAM) has been widely used in the field of information storage and communication. The International Technology Roadmap for Semiconductors (ITRMS) is a 10-year project that was initiated by SObc and the SObc area by 2013 [1]. It leads that the performance of SObc is going to be increasingly up to rely on performance improvement. This paper aims to satisfy the demands of forthcoming SObc performance.<|txt_split|>There are two reasons why the conventional SRAM cells are not sufficient. Firstly, as technology advances, the cost of the SRAM cells increases and the supply chain becomes more manufacturing process, and it deteriorates SRAM's stability. Secondly, external noise easily destroys the data of conventional SRAM cells, because there are direct effects on the internal noise in the SRAM cells, and the conventional SRAM cells in the read operation mode.<|txt_split|>Based on the above two reasons, the high stability of SRAM cell design is essential. Therefore, we propose a new SRAM cell which has high stability enough to satisfy forthcoming SoC performance. Our proposed SRAM architecture is based on the conventional 4T SRAM cell. The main difference in detail why the 4T structure is chosen in the section II.<|txt_split|>The rest of this paper is organized as follows: In Section II, we introduce the main results and the reason why their stability becomes lower when the CMOS technology develops. In Section III, we present the experimental results and the performance comparisons. The read, write and standby cycles will be also described. In Section IV, the simulation results and the performance comparisons are given. Finally, Section V concludes this paper.

subnode.title: I. INTRODUCTION_1
subnode.metadata: SRAM; ITRMS; performance improvement; cost increase; manufacturing process; noise interference; read operation mode
subnode.content: With the advantages of high speed and ease of use, static random access memory (SRAM) has been widely used in the field of information storage and communication. The International Technology Roadmap for Semiconductors (ITRMS) is a 10-year project that was initiated by SObc and the SObc area by 2013 [1]. It leads that the performance of SObc is going to be increasingly up to rely on performance improvement. This paper aims to satisfy the demands of forthcoming SObc performance.There are two reasons why the conventional SRAM cells are not sufficient. Firstly, as technology advances, the cost of the SRAM cells increases and the supply chain becomes more manufacturing process, and it deteriorates SRAM's stability. Secondly, external noise easily destroys the data of conventional SRAM cells, because there are direct effects on the internal noise in the SRAM cells, and the conventional SRAM cells in the read operation mode.

subnode.title: I. INTRODUCTION_2
subnode.metadata: SRAM cell stability; 4T SRAM architecture; CMOS technology impact; read/write/standby cycles; simulation performance comparison
subnode.content: Based on the above two reasons, the high stability of SRAM cell design is essential. Therefore, we propose a new SRAM cell which has high stability enough to satisfy forthcoming SoC performance. Our proposed SRAM architecture is based on the conventional 4T SRAM cell. The main difference in detail why the 4T structure is chosen in the section II.The rest of this paper is organized as follows: In Section II, we introduce the main results and the reason why their stability becomes lower when the CMOS technology develops. In Section III, we present the experimental results and the performance comparisons. The read, write and standby cycles will be also described. In Section IV, the simulation results and the performance comparisons are given. Finally, Section V concludes this paper.

children.title: II. THE CONVENTIONAL SRAM CELL
children.metadata: SRAM evaluation indicators; SRAM cell performance; SRAM suitability for high-performance SoCs
children.content: In this section, at first, we introduce the commonly used indicators to evaluate SRAM's characteristics. In the following sections, we discuss the performance of the SRAM cells using the indicators. At last, the reason why the SRAM cells are suitable for such applications is explained in the forthcoming high-performance SoCs is given.

children.title: A. Indicators to evaluate SRAM's characteristics
children.metadata: Static Noise Margin; Power Consumption; Cell Current; Cell Voltage; Read Stability
children.content: There are four indicators to evaluate SRAM's characteristics: (1) static noise margin (SNM) [V], (2) power consumption [Watt], (3) cell current \(\left[\mu \mathrm{A} / \mathrm{cell}\right]\), (4) cell voltage \(\left[\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{CC}}\right]\), and (5) cell current and readout method to measure the read stability of a SRAM cell.

children.title: B. Basic characteristics of conventional SRAM cell
children.metadata: 6T SRAM cell; 4T SRAM cell; PtNPs; NMOS/PMOS mobility ratio; non-destructive read operation; data retention; area efficiency; power consumption; CMOS technology; SRAM cell design
children.content: Conventional SRAM cells are mainly classified into two groups: 6T SRAM cell and 4T SRAM cell which are composed of 100 nm diameter \(\mathrm{Pt}\) -based PtNPs. We summarize the general characteristics of 6T and 4T SRAM cells: In a 4T SRAM cell, PMOS transistors P1 and N2 are used to realize operation of the SMs. The PMOS transistors N3 and N4 in a 6T SRAM cell. Due to the mobility ratio of the NMOS to the PMOS transistors \(\mu_{\mathrm{M}}\) which is usually around two to three, all the transistors can be connected together. The PMOS transistors in SRAM cell must to guarantee a non-destructive read operation, NMOS driver transistors N1 and N2 must be connected times larger than NMOS access transistors N3 and N4[2].<|txt_split|>4T SRAM cell has higher data retention than 4T SRAM cell has, at the expense of area and power consumption. In addition, it is possible to achieve a high level of data retention by enjoying its advantages. On the other hand, our proposed SRAM cell which is introduced in section III is based on a 4T SRAM cell with an area of \(10\mathrm{mm}^2\) and a power consumption is \(29\%\) lower than that of 6T SRAM cell using \(0.18\mu \mathrm{m}\) CMOS technology[3]. Besides, for the same access speed and comparable SNM, the area of a 4T SRAM cell is \(50 - 65\%\) of the area of a conventional 6T SRAM cell[2].

children.title: TABLEI COMPARISON OF CONVENTIONAL 6T AND 4T SRAM CELLS
children.metadata: SRAM cells; 6T; 4T; parameters; norms
children.content:

children.title: Fig.1.A conventional 6T SRAM cell.
children.metadata: SRAM, 6T, storage node, transistor, circuit diagram
children.content: The image shows a schematic diagram of a conventional 6T SRAM (Static Random-Access Memory) cell, labeled as "Fig. 1". The cell consists of six transistors (P1, P2, N1, N2, N3, N4) and two storage nodes (BL and BLOB), with WL (Word Line) connections. The storage node is highlighted in red, indicating its central role in data retention. The transistors are arranged in a cross-like configuration with capacitors (N3 and N4) connected to ground, forming a bistable circuit for memory storage. Arrows indicate current flow paths, and the overall structure illustrates the fundamental architecture of a 6T SRAM cell used in memory arrays.

children.title: Fig.2.A conventional 4T SRAM cell.
children.metadata: SRAM, 4T, storage node, transistor, circuit diagram
children.content: The image displays a schematic diagram of a conventional 4T (four-transistor) SRAM (Static Random-Access Memory) cell. The circuit consists of two PMOS transistors (P1 and P2) and two NMOS transistors (N1 and N2) arranged in a cross-coupled inverter configuration. The storage node is labeled in red and is located at the center of the cell, connecting the two NMOS transistors. The cell is connected to two word lines (WL and BL) and two bit lines (BL and BLB), indicating its functionality in memory storage. The diagram is labeled as "Fig. 2: A conventional 4T SRAM cell."

subnode.title: B. Basic characteristics of conventional SRAM cell_1
subnode.metadata: 6T SRAM cell; 4T SRAM cell; PtNPs; PMOS transistors; NMOS transistors; mobility ratio; non-destructive read operation
subnode.content: Conventional SRAM cells are mainly classified into two groups: 6T SRAM cell and 4T SRAM cell which are composed of 100 nm diameter \(\mathrm{Pt}\) -based PtNPs. We summarize the general characteristics of 6T and 4T SRAM cells: In a 4T SRAM cell, PMOS transistors P1 and N2 are used to realize operation of the SMs. The PMOS transistors N3 and N4 in a 6T SRAM cell. Due to the mobility ratio of the NMOS to the PMOS transistors \(\mu_{\mathrm{M}}\) which is usually around two to three, all the transistors can be connected together. The PMOS transistors in SRAM cell must to guarantee a non-destructive read operation, NMOS driver transistors N1 and N2 must be connected times larger than NMOS access transistors N3 and N4[2].

subnode.title: B. Basic characteristics of conventional SRAM cell_2
subnode.metadata: 4T SRAM cell; data retention; area efficiency; power consumption; SNM performance; CMOS technology; access speed; SRAM cell design
subnode.content: 4T SRAM cell has higher data retention than 4T SRAM cell has, at the expense of area and power consumption. In addition, it is possible to achieve a high level of data retention by enjoying its advantages. On the other hand, our proposed SRAM cell which is introduced in section III is based on a 4T SRAM cell with an area of \(10\mathrm{mm}^2\) and a power consumption is \(29\%\) lower than that of 6T SRAM cell using \(0.18\mu \mathrm{m}\) CMOS technology[3]. Besides, for the same access speed and comparable SNM, the area of a 4T SRAM cell is \(50 - 65\%\) of the area of a conventional 6T SRAM cell[2].

children.title: C. Flow of conventional SRAM cell in miniaturized process
children.metadata: SNM reduction; miniaturization; low power; voltage \( V_{\mathrm{DS}} \); 6T SRAM; 4T SRAM; common port connection; noise reduction; stability enhancement
children.content: The SNM of 6T and 4T SRAM cells will be decreased to insufficient level in the miniaturized process, because of the low power consumption. The proposed SRAM power source voltage \( V_{\mathrm{DS}} \) becomes smaller. As shown in Fig. 1 and Fig. 2, both of 6T SRAM cell and 4T SRAM cell are connected to the same port. In this operation mode, when the access transistor is on, BL and storage node are directly connected. So the external noise is reduced. The proposed approach can get rid of this path direct path from our proposed SRAM cell in order to improve stability against external noise.

children.title: III. PROPOSED 7T SRAM CELL
children.metadata: 7T SRAM cell; high read stability; 4T SRAM cell; minimal transistor size; write access transistors; standby cycles
children.content: In this section, we propose a new 7T SRAM cell which provides high read stability. Fig.3 shows the proposed cell. The 7T SRAM cell consists of 4T SRAM cells, independent and never interfere mutually. NMOS transistors N1 and N2 form a latch and keep the datum. The 7T SRAM cell is composed of 4T SRAM cells, all the transistors P1 and P2 are write access transistors. Due to our structure is based on the 4T SRAM cell, all the transistors in a 7T SRAM cell can be of minimal size. As shown in Fig.4, we propose a new 7T SRAM cell with 6T SRAM cell. We discuss the write, read and standby<|txt_contd|>cycles in more detail in the subsections III-A, III-B and III-C, respectively.

children.title: A. Write Cycle
children.metadata: BL charging; BLB discharging; W1s set low; V0 charged via P1; V0B discharged via P2; W1s charged high; V0 written as "i"; V0B written as "o"; BLB charged; BL discharged
children.content: To write "i" to the storage node \( V_{0} \) ("0" to \( V_{0B} \), simultaneously), at first, BL and BLB are respectively charged and discharged. In the next, the \( W_{1} \)s are set to low to avoid the possibility of charge transfer from the voltage to keep N5 off. \( V_{0} \) is charged up to high-level by BL through P1, while \( V_{0B} \) is discharged to low-level by BLB through P2. When \( V_{0} \) is charged sufficiently, set to high-level, the \( W_{1} \)s are charged up to high-level, and then, to write "o" to the node \( V_{0} \) ("1" to \( V_{0B} \), simultaneously). BL and BLB are discharged and charged, respectively.

children.title: B. Read Cycle
children.metadata: SRAM cell; read operation; storage node; voltage control; noise endurance; 7T structure; datum isolation; transistor switching
children.content: Given that \(V_{0}\) stores "1", at first, \(W_{\mathrm{L}}\) is set to high to turn P1 and P2 off, while \(W_{\mathrm{R}}\) is maintained at a high voltage to keep N5 on, \(\mathrm{BL}\) is discharged through N3 and N5. Whereas BLB stays high because N4 insulates BLB from the GND. The storage nodes \(V_{0}\) and \(V_{0}^{\prime}\) are stored in the decouple the datum from the BL during a read operation.<|txt_split|>In contrast, when \(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{O}}\) stores "0," as long as turning P1 turns on, the voltage N3 on, the BLB is discharged through N4 and N5.<|txt_split|>The read operation of the proposed SRAM cell is different from that of the 6T and 4T SRAM cells. In 7T SRAM cells, the read operation is performed only once. Reading datum does not interfere the storage nodes. Besides, the voltage of the storage node, which stores "0", is closely maintained at the ground level during a period of time. The 4T SRAM cell has higher endurance against external noise.

subnode.title: B. Read Cycle_1
subnode.metadata: BL discharge; V0 storage; P1/P2 control; N3/N4/N5 operation; BLB insulation; read operation; datum decoupling
subnode.content: Given that \(V_{0}\) stores "1", at first, \(W_{\mathrm{L}}\) is set to high to turn P1 and P2 off, while \(W_{\mathrm{R}}\) is maintained at a high voltage to keep N5 on, \(\mathrm{BL}\) is discharged through N3 and N5. Whereas BLB stays high because N4 insulates BLB from the GND. The storage nodes \(V_{0}\) and \(V_{0}^{\prime}\) are stored in the decouple the datum from the BL during a read operation.In contrast, when \(\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{O}}\) stores "0," as long as turning P1 turns on, the voltage N3 on, the BLB is discharged through N4 and N5.

subnode.title: B. Read Cycle_2
subnode.metadata: SRAM cell read operation; 7T SRAM; noise endurance; storage node voltage; datum reading interference
subnode.content: The read operation of the proposed SRAM cell is different from that of the 6T and 4T SRAM cells. In 7T SRAM cells, the read operation is performed only once. Reading datum does not interfere the storage nodes. Besides, the voltage of the storage node, which stores "0", is closely maintained at the ground level during a period of time. The 4T SRAM cell has higher endurance against external noise.

children.title: C. Standby Cycle
children.metadata: 4T SRAM cell; H mode leakage current; I_Lax current; bitline capacitance; stable operation; higher SNM; 7T SRAM design
children.content: Due to our structure is based on the 4T SRAM cell, the data is generated by a single-cell device. The data is the "H" mode data potential is gradually lost by the leakage current through N1 or N2, this current is named as the "H" mode current. In the case of SRAM cells, we employ the method to retain the datum by other<|txt_contd|>leakage current which flows through P1. This current is named \( I_{\mathrm{Lax}} \). To let \( I_{\mathrm{Lax}} \) flow into P1 or P2, BL and \( I_{\mathrm{Lax}} \) are connected to \( I_{\mathrm{Lax}} \). The current is assumed to be "H" to turn P1 and P2 off. To retain "H", it is required that \( I_{\mathrm{Lax}} = 2I_{\mathrm{Lax}} \). As \( I_{\mathrm{Lax}} \) varies largely by manufacturing<|txt_contd|>variation, we usually design \(\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{intetation}}\) so that it is more than hundred times of \(\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{sub}}\) [4].<|txt_split|>The advantage of our SRAM cell is providing the stable operations, and improving the higher SNM than the 4T and 6T SRAMs. The main advantage of our drawback of our SRAM cell is the problem of bitline capacitance. In general, the bitline capacitance is composed of the metal capacitance of the bitline and the gate capacitance of the SRAM. Because our separate data-reading access mechanism, the 7T SRAM have greater metal capacitance of the bitline than SRAMs, and the higher capacitance as compared to the 4T and 6T SRAMs.

children.title: BL Fig.4. The data retention in the standby cycle.
children.metadata: Electronics circuit diagram; standby mode; data retention; leakage current; transistor
children.content: The diagram illustrates the data retention mechanism in a standby cycle, showing a circuit with two transistors labeled "H" (high) and a diode labeled "L" (low). An arrow labeled "I_retention" indicates the current flowing through the circuit, while "I_leak" denotes the leakage current from the diode. The label "BL" is positioned beneath the diode, likely indicating a battery or logic level. The diagram is titled "Fig. 4. The data retention in the standby cycle."

subnode.title: C. Standby Cycle_1
subnode.metadata: 4T SRAM cell; H mode data loss; leakage current; I_Lax; P1/P2 retention; manufacturing variation
subnode.content: Due to our structure is based on the 4T SRAM cell, the data is generated by a single-cell device. The data is the "H" mode data potential is gradually lost by the leakage current through N1 or N2, this current is named as the "H" mode current. In the case of SRAM cells, we employ the method to retain the datum by otherleakage current which flows through P1. This current is named \( I_{\mathrm{Lax}} \). To let \( I_{\mathrm{Lax}} \) flow into P1 or P2, BL and \( I_{\mathrm{Lax}} \) are connected to \( I_{\mathrm{Lax}} \). The current is assumed to be "H" to turn P1 and P2 off. To retain "H", it is required that \( I_{\mathrm{Lax}} = 2I_{\mathrm{Lax}} \). As \( I_{\mathrm{Lax}} \) varies largely by manufacturing

subnode.title: C. Standby Cycle_2
subnode.metadata: SRAM cell design; high SNM advantage; bitline capacitance issue; metal capacitance increase; 7T SRAM structure
subnode.content: variation, we usually design \(\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{intetation}}\) so that it is more than hundred times of \(\mathrm{I}_{\mathrm{sub}}\) [4].The advantage of our SRAM cell is providing the stable operations, and improving the higher SNM than the 4T and 6T SRAMs. The main advantage of our drawback of our SRAM cell is the problem of bitline capacitance. In general, the bitline capacitance is composed of the metal capacitance of the bitline and the gate capacitance of the SRAM. Because our separate data-reading access mechanism, the 7T SRAM have greater metal capacitance of the bitline than SRAMs, and the higher capacitance as compared to the 4T and 6T SRAMs.

children.title: Fig.3.Proposed 7T SRAM cell
children.metadata: SRAM cell; 7T; transistor; circuit diagram
children.content: The image displays a schematic diagram of a proposed 7T SRAM (Static Random-Access Memory) cell, labeled as Fig. 3. The circuit consists of seven transistors (P1, P2, N1, N2, N3, N4, N5) and two nodes (VIO and VIOB) connected to power lines (BL and BLB) and a word line (WLw). The transistors are arranged to form cross-coupled inverters (N1 and N2) with additional transistors (N3, N4, N5) for write/read functionality, including a resistor (Rw) connected between N3 and N4. The diagram illustrates the logic and structure of the memory cell, emphasizing its use in non-volatile or low-power storage applications.

subnode.title: III. PROPOSED 7T SRAM CELL_1
subnode.metadata: 7T SRAM cell; high read stability; 4T SRAM cell; latch structure; write access transistors; minimal size transistors; 6T SRAM cell; write-read-standby operation
subnode.content: In this section, we propose a new 7T SRAM cell which provides high read stability. Fig.3 shows the proposed cell. The 7T SRAM cell consists of 4T SRAM cells, independent and never interfere mutually. NMOS transistors N1 and N2 form a latch and keep the datum. The 7T SRAM cell is composed of 4T SRAM cells, all the transistors P1 and P2 are write access transistors. Due to our structure is based on the 4T SRAM cell, all the transistors in a 7T SRAM cell can be of minimal size. As shown in Fig.4, we propose a new 7T SRAM cell with 6T SRAM cell. We discuss the write, read and standby

The layout includes multiple peripheral blocks arranged in a symmetrical pattern around the core, with labeled rows and columns indicating connection points or functional zones. The outermost layer contains a series of rectangular modules, likely representing input/output pads or interface circuits. children.content: The image displays a detailed physical layout of a proposed 7T cell, which appears to be a semiconductor device structure. It consists of multiple rectangular blocks and interconnected pathways, arranged in a vertical and horizontal configuration. The layout features distinct colored lines—yellow, red, and blue—indicating different types of interconnects or functional regions. The structure is enclosed within a boundary, suggesting a defined chip area, with labeled sections at the top and bottom, possibly indicating input/output regions or specific functional modules. children.title: Fig. 8. SNM definition used in the analytical SNM expression for load-less SRAM cell[2].
children.metadata: SRAM, SNM, analytical expression, load-less, voltage, transfer characteristics
children.content: The image is a graph illustrating the definition of the Supply Noise Margin (SNM) for a load-less SRAM cell, as indicated by the caption. The graph plots voltage (V_out) on the y-axis against input voltage (V_in) on the x-axis, showing transfer characteristics with regions marked as NM_L (lower noise margin), NM_H (higher noise margin), and NM (total noise margin). The analytical SNM expression is also indicated. The graph includes labeled voltage thresholds (V_th, V_t, V_in, V_out) and regions for both NM_L and NM_H, with a shaded area representing the SNM region. The diagram highlights the relationship between input and output voltages for the SRAM cell under load-less conditions.

children.title: Fig.9.The SNM curve of the 4T SRAM cell, given TT model, \( T = 25^{\circ}\mathrm{C} \) \( V_{\mathrm{DD}} = 1.8\mathrm{V} \)
children.metadata: SRAM, 4T, TT model, T=25°C, VDD=1.8V
children.content: The image displays a graph titled "Fig. 9. The SNM curve of the 4T SRAM cell, given TT model, T = 25°C, VDD = 1.8 V." The curve, plotted in red, illustrates the Supply Noise Margin (SNM) as a function of voltage, showing a characteristic S-shaped curve with a labeled point indicating 0.55 V. The graph has a grid background and includes axes labeled for voltage (x-axis) and SNM (y-axis).

children.title: Fig. 10. The SNM curve of the 6T SRAM cell, given TT model, \( T = 25^{\circ}\mathrm{C} \), \( V_{\mathrm{DD}} = 1.8\mathrm{V} \).
children.metadata: SRAM cell, SNM curve, TT model, temperature 25°C, VDD 1.8 V
children.content: The image displays a graph labeled as "Fig. 10. The SNM curve of the 6T SRAM cell, given TT model, T = 25°C, VDD = 1.8 V." The x-axis represents time (in nanoseconds), and the y-axis represents voltage (in volts). The curve shows a steep initial drop in voltage followed by a gradual decline, with a peak labeled "1.09V" at the start of the curve. The graph is plotted on a grid with fine lines, indicating precise measurements. The overall shape suggests the behavior of the SRAM cell under specified conditions.

children.title: Fig. 11. The SNM curve of the 7T SRAM cell, given TT model, \( T = 25\mathrm{C} \), \( V_{\mathrm{DD}} = 1.8\mathrm{V} \).
children.metadata: SRAM, TT model, 25°C, VDD=1.8 V
children.content: The image displays a graph labeled "Fig. 11. The SNM curve of the 7T SRAM cell, given TT model, T = 25°C, VDD = 1.8 V." The graph plots SNM (Switching Noise Margin) against voltage on the x-axis and y-axis, respectively. A red line shows a sharp drop in SNM at approximately 1.43 V, indicating the threshold voltage where the cell transitions from stable to unstable operation. A black line represents a slower, more gradual decline in SNM at lower voltages. The gridlines and labeled axes help quantify the behavior of the SRAM cell under the specified conditions.

subnode.title: IV. SIMULATION RESULTS_1
subnode.metadata: SRAM cell simulation; 0.18μm CMOS process; chip area 0.95mm²; 128-bit layout; Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company; Writedelay performance; chip width 0.93mm; physical layout; Table II data
subnode.content: The simulation results of SRAM cells are demonstrated in this section with the emphasis on the performance characteristics of the proposed device (Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company) 1-Poly 6-Metals \(0.18\mathrm{-}\mu \mathrm{m}\) CMOS process. Fig.5 shows that the chip area is \(0.95\mathrm{mm}^2\) and the chip width is \(0.93\mathrm{mm}\) with correct operation. The chip area with pads is \(0.958^{*}8\) \(0.923\mathrm{mm}\) with 128bit including the peripheral circuits. The whole chip layout and the physical layout of the chip are shown in Fig.6, respectively. Table II shows that the manufacturing process, power supply voltage, power consumption and the duration tests are Writedelay, Writedelay, and Writedelay, respectively.

subnode.title: IV. SIMULATION RESULTS_2
subnode.metadata: SRAM read stability; static noise margin; butterfly graph; noise margin definition; loadless SRAM cell
subnode.content: For the read stability of SRAM, static noise margin (SNM) is the standard method to measure the read stability of a SRAM cell. It can be measured graphically from the butterfly graph. The butterfly graph is a plot of the read signal and the corresponding noise margin. Although, several definitions of the SNM can be found in the current textbooks, one standard SNM definition is not commonly used yet. According to [2], the SNM of a loadless SRAM cell as the diagonal of the rectangle with the read signal \( \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{m}} \) and the read signal \( \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{m}}^{\prime} \) and the noise margin low \( (\mathrm{NM}_{\mathrm{L}}) \), as shown in Fig.8, \( \mathrm{NM}_{\mathrm{L}} \) and \( \mathrm{NM}_{\mathrm{R}} \) are defined as Equations 1 and 2, respectively:

subnode.title: IV. SIMULATION RESULTS_4
subnode.metadata: SRAM cell; butterfly curves; read SNM; 4T SRAM; 6T SRAM; 7T SM cell; simulation results; operating environment; SNM enhancement

children.title: V. CONCLUSION
children.metadata: SRAM cell; 7T structure; read operation; high stability; Read SNM; 0.18μm CMOS; 4T SRAM; 6T SRAM
children.content: We propose a new SRAM cell, which offers high stability for read operations. The new 7T SRAM cell is consisted of two separate data access mechanisms: one is for the read operation and another is for the write one. In this paper, we investigate the performance of the proposed storage node during the reading operation. The SRAM cell is simulated in \(0.18\mu \mathrm{m}\) CMOS technology.<|txt_split|>The Read SNM is as high as \(1.43\mathrm{V}\) at \(\mathrm{Y_{IN}} = 1.8\mathrm{V}.\) It is found that the Read SNM is significantly enhanced by \(1.6\mathrm{X}\) and \(0.31\mathrm{X}\) as compared to the conventional 4T and 6T SRAM cell respectively.

subnode.title: A New 7-Transistor SRAM Cell Design with High Read Stability_1
subnode.metadata: Seven-transistor SRAM; Noise immunity; Double word operation; Static-Noise-Margin; Data destruction prevention
subnode.content: Yen Hsiang Tseng, Yimeng Zhang, Leona Okamura and Tsutomu YoshiharaGraduate School of Information, Production and Systems, Waseda University, Fukuoka, 808-0135, JapanEmail: yimeng.zhang@fuji.waseda.jpAbstract-The conventional SRAMs, namely four-transistor SRAM (4T) and six-transistor SRAM (6T), suffered from the external noise, because they have direct paths through the external impedance. In this study, we have studied a seven-transistor (7T) SRAM which has no direct path through BL to the data storage nodes and has higher endurance against external noise. The proposed cell is composed of two separate data access mechanisms; one is a 12-bit double word operation (DWord) and the other is based upon our SRAM design, data destruction never occurs in the read operation. Simulation result shows that the read Static-Noise-Margin (SMM) of the proposed cell was enhanced by LAX and 8.31X with the conventional 4T and 6T systems. This results also manufactured a cell and confirmed its performance.
